audio_basics.title: Audio Basics
audio_basics.content:
Sound in computers is just numbers changing really fast! Here's what you need to know:

• Sample Rate - How many times per second we measure the sound (usually 44100 or 48000)
• Channels - Separate audio streams (stereo = 2 channels: left and right)
• Volume - How loud the sound is (0% to 100%, sometimes higher)

Pipeflow lets you route and control all of this visually.
audio_basics.tip: Most audio is stereo (2 channels), but surround sound can have 5.1 or 7.1 channels!

volume_control.title: Volume Control
volume_control.content:
Every audio node can have its volume adjusted:

🔊 Master Volume - Overall level for the node
📊 Per-Channel - Individual control over left, right, etc.
🔇 Mute - Complete silence without changing volume settings

Volume goes from 0% (silent) to 200% (boosted). 100% is unity gain - the original level.
volume_control.tip: Boosting above 100% can cause distortion. If audio sounds crunchy, try lowering the volume.

understanding_meters.title: Understanding Meters
understanding_meters.content:
The animated bars on nodes show audio levels:

🟢 Green - Good levels, nice and healthy
🟡 Yellow - Getting loud, watch it
🔴 Red - Too hot! Might distort

The meter shows two things:
• Peak - The loudest moment (fast, jumpy)
• RMS - Average loudness (smoother, represents perceived volume)
understanding_meters.tip: Aim for green with occasional yellow. Constant red means something's too loud!

link_flow_visualization.title: Connection Activity
link_flow_visualization.content:
Links between nodes light up when audio flows through them! This helps you:

• See which connections are actually carrying signal
• Trace the path of audio through your setup
• Find 'dead' connections that aren't working

Brighter glow = more audio activity.
link_flow_visualization.tip: If a link doesn't light up when you expect audio, the source might be muted or silent.

media_classes.title: Media Classes Explained
media_classes.content:
PipeWire categorizes devices by what they do:

🔊 Audio/Sink - Plays sound (speakers, headphones)
🎤 Audio/Source - Captures sound (microphones)
🔀 Audio/Duplex - Does both (audio interfaces)
🎬 Video/Source - Captures video (cameras, screen share)
🎹 Midi/Bridge - Musical instrument data

This helps you quickly identify what each node does.
media_classes.tip: The icon on each node gives you a quick visual hint about its media class.

channels_explained.title: Channels & Ports
channels_explained.content:
Each audio stream can have multiple channels:

• Mono - 1 channel (basic microphone)
• Stereo - 2 channels (FL=Front Left, FR=Front Right)
• Surround - 5.1 has 6 channels, 7.1 has 8

Each channel becomes a port on the node. You can connect them individually for complex routing!
channels_explained.tip: Connecting stereo to mono? The channels will be mixed together automatically.

latency.title: About Latency
latency.content:
Latency is the delay between input and output. Lower is better, especially for:

• Live music performance
• Voice chat
• Gaming

PipeWire is designed for low latency. If you experience delay, check your audio settings or buffer sizes.
latency.tip: High latency usually comes from applications, not PipeWire itself.
